coredata.dc:title: On the modeling and simulation of a stratospheric experiment power subsystem
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of IAA.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of IAA.
coredata.dc:creator: Marín-Coca, S.
González-Bárcena, D.
Roibás-Millán, E.
Pindado, S.
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes an accurate model of the Electrical Power Subsystem (EPS) of the Thermal Analysis Support and Environment Characterization Laboratory (TASEC-Lab), a university experiment developed for using in high-altitude balloon missions to measure and analyze the convection heat transfer. A Li-ion battery and two high efficient DC–DC converters have been characterized through laboratory tests and fitting to the experimental results. The developed mathematical models described in this paper are used to carry out accurate EPS simulations and are validated by comparison with the experimental data.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Power subsystem
Stratospheric balloon
Li-ion battery
DC–DC converter
Parameter estimation
Least-squares

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TASEC-Lab during the pre-launch activities (a) and integration of the TASEC-Lab EPS (b).
TASEC-Lab power subsystem simplified electrical circuit.
Estimated power consumption, P , on the ＋5 V and ＋12 V lines during the TASEC-Lab first mission (July 2021).
Battery equivalent circuit during a discharging process at constant rate.

Typical Li-ion battery discharging curve. Battery output voltage, V , is plotted as a function of the discharged energy, ϕ , for three discharging currents such
I
1
>
I
2
>
I
3
. The three areas in which the battery shows different behavior (that is, different evolution of the voltage level) are indicated in the graph.

Evolution of the battery output voltage, V , as a function of the discharged energy, ϕ , for different discharging current rates. Experimental results are plotted with solid lines and the fitting curves with dashed lines. The results of the linear model described in [19,27] and the obtained with the new model from the present research are plotted in (a) and (b), respectively.

Efficiency of the DC–DC converters as a function of the output current for an input voltages of 18 V, 20 V, 22 V and 24 V. The fitting curves are plotted with solid lines and the experimental data with symbols.

Efficiency of the he 5 V DC–DC converter as a function of the output current for an input voltage of 20 V. The experimental data is plotted with points and the fitting curves of the different models in solid lines.
Efficiency of the 12 V DC–DC converter as a function of the input voltage for different output currents .
Functional test of the Electronical Power System of TASEC-Lab.
Evolution of the battery voltage during the test (dotted line) and the simulation (solid line).
Simulated power dissipation of the Electrical Power Subsystem (EPS) and the contribution of the converters and battery to this power.
Parameters of the proposed battery discharging model.

Estimation of R through the voltage and current drop,
Δ V
and
Δ I
, between different discharging curves. The voltage and current increments have been obtained between the 1C discharging curve and the others, one at a time (0.1C–1C, 0.25C–1C, 0.5C–1C). The selected voltage of each curve corresponds to when the discharged energy reaches half the maximum (
ϕ ≈ 30
W h).
Voltage Root-Mean-Square Error (RMSE) of the linear [19,27] battery discharging model and the new one described in the present research, for different discharging current rates.
Fitting parameters of the 12 V and 5 V DC–DC converters efficiency model.
Root-Mean-Square Error (RMSE) of the 12 V and 5 V DC–DC converters efficiency model, for an input voltage of 18 V, 20 V, 22 V and 24 V.

head.ce:title: On the modeling and simulation of a stratospheric experiment power subsystem

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universidad Politécnica de Madrid, Instituto Universitario de Microgravedad ”Ignacio Da Riva” (IDR/UPM), ETSI Aeronáutica y del Espacio, Plaza del Cardenal Cisneros 3, 28040 Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidad Politécnica de Madrid, Instituto Universitario de Microgravedad ”Ignacio Da Riva” (IDR/UPM), ETSI Aeronáutica y del Espacio

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Universidad Politénica de Madrid, Instituto Universitario de Microgravedad ”Ignacio Da Riva” (IDR/UPM), ETSI Aeronáutica y del Espacio, Plaza del Cardenal Cisneros 3, 28040 Madrid, Spain.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes an accurate model of the Electrical Power Subsystem (EPS) of the Thermal Analysis Support and Environment Characterization Laboratory (TASEC-Lab), a university experiment developed for using in high-altitude balloon missions to measure and analyze the convection heat transfer. A Li-ion battery and two high efficient DC–DC converters have been characterized through laboratory tests and fitting to the experimental results. The developed mathematical models described in this paper are used to carry out accurate EPS simulations and are validated by comparison with the experimental data.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Converter output current at maximum efficiency

ce:section.ce:para: The TASEC-Lab project consists of a university experiment that has been recently flown (July 2021) in a stratospheric balloon (see Fig. 1(a)). Its main objective is to characterize the convective heat transfer on board this kind of platforms, which are becoming a very recurrent solution for testing technologies before going to space [1]. The environmental conditions above 20 km (which are very similar to those in space), together with the economical advantages and the possibility of recovering the payload after the flight, make these platforms a suitable way for this purpose. However, they are not only used as testing platforms, but also as scientific platforms themselves. Many missions have been launched on board stratospheric balloons to study the Earth, the atmosphere, or even the Sun [2–5]. Since the floating altitude is above 99% of the atmosphere and they have a constant view of the Sun, these platforms are very attractive to the scientific community.

The Instituto Universitario de Microgravedad “Ignacio Da Riva” (IDR/UPM Institute) has participated in some stratospheric balloon missions such as SUNRISE [2], since its first flight in 2008. Staff from the IDR/UPM Institute have been responsible for the thermal control subsystem, they having been in charge of the thermal design and analysis [6]. Their work during these years arises some limitations when modeling the convective heat transfer during the flight, which motivates the analysis of this phenomenon using stratospheric balloon platforms. This kind of missions are usually analyzed as space mission does. Although at the floating altitude the conductive and radiative heat transfer are the main heat transfer mechanisms, the convective heat transfer is not negligible under certain conditions [7,8].

TASEC-Lab has been designed for studying the convective heat transfer in high-altitude balloon missions, in order to obtain new correlations which will allow the thermal engineers to improve their models [9]. As responsible for the Master’s Degree in Space Systems of the Universidad Politécnica de Madrid (UPM), the IDR/UPM Institute decided to turn the TASEC-Lab into a university project by involving Bachelor, Master and Ph.D. students. The experiment has been completely designed, integrated, and tested by these students at the IDR/UPM Institute facilities. The development process of this experiment has been similar to the one involved in a satellite mission, following the same phases but using Commercial-Of-The-Shelf (COTS) components to achieve a low cost design.

The mission concept proposed by B2Space (the company in charge of the gondola design and the operation of the launch) was to get an autonomous experiment in terms of electrical power. For that reason, the Electrical Power Subsystem (EPS) of TASEC-Lab experiment is one of the most important, even critical, subsystems. It is responsible for providing and distributing the electrical power using only a battery as the main power supply. The flight was expected to last up to 8 h, so no additional power source would be needed.

To ensure a robust design of a stratospheric balloon’s EPS, its components have to be tested and modeled. This procedure makes it possible to guarantee a proper performance of the EPS during the mission. To the authors’ knowledge and based on the available literature, there seems to be a lack regarding published works on power subsystem simulations of stratospheric balloon missions. Nevertheless, some information can be found related to the performances of batteries and DC–DC converters in these missions [10–14].

In this project, based on the expertise of the IDR/UPM Institute in this field [15–24], a numerical simulator of the EPS of TASEC-Lab has been modeled to predict its performance (i.e., battery voltage and power dissipation) during operation. For this purpose, several laboratory tests have been conducted and a set of experimental data has been used to develop analytical models of the main electrical parts: a Li-ion battery and two DC–DC converters. The work included in this paper describes how the EPS of TASEC-Lab has been designed, simulated, and tested, and may help other researchers and scientists to simulate similar EPS’s within the development of stratospheric balloon missions.
The main contributions of this paper are:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Development of a novel Li-lion battery discharging model.
Formulation of a new buck DC/DC converter efficiency model.
Simulation and model validation of the electrical power subsystem of a stratospheric balloon experiment.

ce:section.ce:para: TASEC-Lab Power Subsystem is described in Section 2, and the modeling of the different components are detailed in Section 3. This model has been validated based on an estimated power profile as described in Section 3. Finally, the conclusions of this work are summarized in Section 4.

TASEC-Lab power subsystem (see Fig. 1(b)), is designed with two main power lines with different voltage levels and power requirements. One line supplies power at ＋5 V to the On-Board Computer (OBC), a Raspberry Pi 3B+. The OBC, in turn, feeds secondary power lines at ＋5 V and ＋3.3 V. The second main line is powered at ＋12 V and supplies power to a cup anemometer (see Fig. 1(a)), its heater, and the heat transfer experiment to be carried out during the balloon flight.

The EPS of the TASEC-Lab is composed by a Li-ion battery, two DC–DC converters, a distribution harness, and a mechanical switch to turn on the system (see Fig. 2). As the main power lines have different input voltages, and the voltage level of the battery changes with its State Of Charge (SOC), a DC–DC converter per line is needed. The selected DC–DC converters are compact and highly efficient buck converters [25]. Additionally, to ensure a good margin of remaining energy after the TASEC-Lab missions, the EPS carries a 24 V 2900 mA h Samsung Li-ion battery [26]. Regarding the mechanical switch, it is made through a D-SUB 9 connector, which is attached to the lower tray and it is connected in series to the EPS bus line. To turn on the system, the anemometer is connected from the outside, closing the circuit between the battery and the DC–DC converters. This connector also allows the operation of the experiment without the anemometer during the tests or the battery charging.

As said in Section 1, the proper modeling of the EPS is an essential phase of the TASEC-Lab project. On the one hand, it helps to ensure that the battery is well-sized and supply enough power during the mission. On the other hand, the power losses affect the thermal behavior of the TASEC-Lab experiment.

Several electrical and thermal tests have been performed simulating the electrical consumption and thermal environment of the first TASEC-Lab flight, respectively. Additionally, TASEC-Lab is expected to perform additional flights, and the following missions or even the payloads might differ from one to another. Therefore, an EPS full model is essential to predict the electrical and thermal behavior of TASEC-Lab in future missions.

The EPS modeling starts by analyzing the power consumption of the ＋5 V and ＋12 V lines. This is done by defining the operative modes of TASEC-Lab, which consists in detailing for each power line which components are turned on and its consumption at any time of the mission. An estimation of the power consumption of TASEC-Lab is shown in Fig. 3. On the one hand, there is a steady power consumption corresponding to the OBC, electronic sensors and the thermal heaters. On the other hand, there is a variable consumption on the ＋12 V line corresponding to the Heat Transfer Laboratory, which operates in different modes depending on the expected thermal scenario. The next step is to model the discharging performance of the battery to predict its voltage level or SOC during its operation. This is a key factor to determine if the battery is capable of supplying enough energy without exceeding safe margins of the remaining capacity. Then, it is necessary to model the DC–DC converters, as a good estimation of their power losses would improve the thermal model of TASEC-Lab. Finally, the EPS modeling ends by coupling the developed models in a single one using simple energy conservation and electrical principles.

The selected battery composed of 6 Li-ion cells in series has a nominal capacity of 2900 mA h, that is, the battery lasts approximately 1 h being discharged at 2.9 A. However, TASEC-Lab is designed to operate several hours and the demanded current is variable along the flight. Therefore, several battery discharging tests were performed to characterize its behavior at different discharge rates. As it will be discussed later, the TASEC-Lab demanded battery current evolves smoothly, with only a few current discontinuities (when the operational mode of the TASEC-Lab changes). Hence, it can be reasonably assumed that the dynamic response time is negligible in relation to the total mission time. For these reasons, a good characterization of the battery discharging process can be achieved considering a quasi-stationary discharge.

A total of four battery discharging tests were carried out using the Mayuno M9812 electronic load controlled by Mayuno M9711 software. Starting from 25.2 V of Open Circuit Voltage (OCV), and setting a cut-off voltage of 16.2 V, the battery was discharged at a constant rate of 1C, 0.5C, 0.25C and 0.1C (i.e., 2.9 A, 1.45 A, 0.745 A and 0.29 A). During the tests, the battery voltage, current, and elapsed time was measured and recorded.

The test data were used to formulate a new equivalent circuit model (see Fig. 4) for the characterization of the discharging battery performance. This model is based on the energy discharge level model developed at the IDR/UPM Institute [19,27]. In that model, three important assumptions are made: (1) internal energy losses (excluding joule losses) are neglected, (2) internal resistance is constant, (3) temperature and aging (cycling) effects are not considered. Taking into account the aforementioned hypothesis, the output voltage level of the battery, V , 1
1 See Nomenclature at top of paper. is calculated as:
(1)
V = E
ϕ , I
− I R
,
where E is the internal voltage, I is the discharging current and R is the internal resistance. The internal voltage, E , is assumed to be a function of the discharging current, and the discharged energy, ϕ , is defined as follows:
(2)
ϕ
t
=
ϕ
0
+
∫
t
0
t
V I + R
I
2
d t
,
where
ϕ
0
is the initial discharged energy, and
t
0
and t are the initial and final times of the discharging process. In [19,27], the internal voltage, E , is modeled as a linear/exponential function and the battery voltage, V , fits quite well to the experimental data. However, the authors have found some limitations of that model. In the first place, that model does not take into account the initial exponential decay. Even this discharging zone represents a low energy in comparison to the total available discharging energy of the battery, it is not negligible in accurate simulations. Additionally, that model consists of many parameters to be optimized. For these reasons, the following accurate model for the internal voltage, E , is proposed:
(3)
E
ϕ , I
=
V
max
−
V
max
−
V
min
+ I R
y
ϕ , I
,
where
V
max
and
V
min
are the maximum and minimum battery voltage levels and y is a fitting function.
V
max
and
V
min
can be extracted from the manufacture’s datasheet or can be estimated knowing that a typical Li-ion cell has an operational voltage range between 2.7 V and 4.2 V, respectively. Regarding function y , it takes values between 0 and 1. When the battery is fully charged, y is equal 0 and the internal battery voltage, E , is equal to the maximum voltage,
V
max
. On the other hand, when the battery is fully discharged, y is equal to 1 and E is equal to the minimum battery voltage
V
min
+ I R
. In this case, and according to Eq. (1), the battery voltage V is equal to
V
min
.

Function y has been built according to the three representative zones of the discharging curve (see Fig. 5). Each one of these zones is modeled with an exponential function:
(4)
y
ϕ , I
= A
e
− a x

x
and
C
e
− c
1 − x
model the first exponential decay, the linear zone and the second exponential decay, respectively. Additionally, a , b and c parameters are functions of the discharging current, I , and x is the ratio between the discharged energy and the maximum discharging energy:
(5)
x =

.
Moreover, A , B and C coefficients are determined by imposing the following boundary conditions:
(6)

|

(7)

− 1

(8)

.

The first boundary condition (Eq. (6)) establishes that the exponential function y is 0 when the discharged energy is null. Regarding the secondary boundary condition (Eq. (7)), y drops to an empirical value of 0.1 when the discharging process starts a linear decrease (i.e., when the dimensionless discharged energy, x , reaches a value of
a
− 1
). The last boundary condition (Eq. (8)) implies that y is 1 when the battery has discharged its maximum available energy. Imposing these boundary conditions to Eq. (4) leads to the following linear system:
(9)
1

.

Additionally, resistance R is assumed to be constant, and a first estimate of its value has been obtained by averaging the resistances at the beginning of the discharging tests (i.e. dividing the initial voltage drop by the current):
(10)
R =
1
n
∑
i = 1
n
V
max
− V
|
ϕ = 0 , I =
I
i
I
i
,
where
I
i
is the discharging current of the i -discharging test and n is the number of discharging tests.

In Fig. 6, the battery voltage (calculated with both the linear model and the proposed model) is plotted as a function of the discharged energy. The parameters of the new model are included in Table 1. The voltage parameters
V
max
and
V
min
have been extracted from the manufacturer datasheet. An initial value of R was calculated with Eq. (10) and was useful in calculating the discharged energy as a function of the time,
ϕ
( t )
, (see Eq. (2)) for each discharging rate. However, R is obtained from a transient state and could not be very representative of the discharging tests as they can be considered as quasi-static processes. For this reason, this value was validated with the well-known procedure consisting of analyzing the voltages and currents drop (at the same SoC) between a pair of discharging curves. The results of this procedure, shown in Table 2, demonstrate for this particular battery that the estimate of R with Eq. (2) is sufficiently accurate. The maximum discharged energy,
ϕ
max
, has been calculated with Eq. (2) for different current rates and has been approximated by a 3rd-degree polynomial function. On the other hand, a , b and c parameters have been estimated by applying the Least Square Method (LSM) to each experimental curve. Finally, these parameters have been approximated by quadratic and linear functions of the discharging current. As Fig. 6 shows, the discharging curves of the proposed model are very similar to the experimental ones. The larger variations are located in the linear zone, where the experimental data show some undulations. Nevertheless, the accuracy of the model is confirmed by the small values of the battery voltage Root-Mean-Square Error (RMSE). These values are presented in Table 3 along with the ones obtained with the linear model described in the previous work [19,27].

TASEC-Lab EPS carries two high-efficient and low-power DC–DC buck converters manufactured by RECOM (models R-78T5 and R-78T12). These are commercial switching regulator buck converters with efficiencies up to 95%. In the present paper, the converters are analyzed from a systemic point of view, i.e., studying their performance in relation to the power distribution system. More information on DC–DC converters can be found in [28–30]. Their datasheets [25] include their efficiency curves as a function of the output current for different input voltages. These curves help to understand the behavior of the converters, but their characterization are limited to a few input voltages. In the case of the R-78T5 model (5 V output) these voltages are 42, 36, 24, 12 and 8 V, whereas the efficiency curves from R-78T12 model (12 V output) are shown for 42, 36, 24, and 15 V input voltage levels. Since the converters are connected in parallel to the battery, whose nominal voltage is between 25.2 V and 16.2 V, only the 24 V datasheet curve provides useful data. For that reason, the authors found mandatory to perform an accurate characterization of the converters when operating at the battery voltage range, as it would allow an estimation of the power losses and the total battery consumption.

Both DC–DC converters have been tested following the electrical circuit shown in Fig. 7. It consists of one DC power source, four multimeters, an electronic load, and a DC–DC converter. This configuration allows accurate measurements of the input and output voltages and currents of the DC–DC converters.

During the tests, the power source supplied a constant voltage (measured by the input voltmeter) of 24 V, 22 V, 20 V, and 18 V. Additionally, for each input voltage, the electronic load was configured to demand a current (through the ammeter) from the minimum (0.1 A) to the maximum (1 A) operative output current [25] in steps of 0.05 A.

In total, 76 measurements of input and output current and voltage were collected for each of the two DC–DC converters. The efficiencies of the converters were calculated according to Eq. (11). To give a graphical overview of the tests, the efficiencies are plotted as a function of the output current (for each input voltage) in Fig. 8.
(11)
η =
V
out
I
out
V
in
I
in
.

According to Fig. 8, the efficiency of both DC–DC converters rapidly increases with the current until it reaches a maximum, and then a quasi-linear decrease is followed. In both DC–DC converters, the efficiency is maximized at a particular current value. For example, the 5 V DC–DC converter presents its higher values for output currents around 0.7 A, meanwhile the 12 V DC–DC is more efficient supplying a current of 0.6 A. Additionally, the efficiency curves of each DC–DC converter seem to be displaced between each other with an approximate constant vertical offset. This means that the difference between two efficiency curves of the same DC–DC converter is not affected by the output current. By analyzing the data, it can be concluded that the offset between the curves has a linear dependence with the input voltage. For instance, the linear regressions of the 12 V DC–DC converter efficiency curves show the same slope, being approximately −0.0025 V−1 (see Fig. 10). The overall behavior of the efficiency could be approximated with a simple quadratic function. However, as the efficiency curves do not have a perfect horizontal symmetry, non-negligible errors could arise for output currents higher than the optimum. Different efficiency models have been implemented to select the one that best reproduces the behavior of the DC–DC converters. In the first model, here called “Exponential” and developed at the IDR/UPM Institute [31], the efficiency of the converter is a function of the output current (see Eq. (12)) and is independent of the input voltage. This model is described by the maximum efficiency,
η
max
, and an unknown polynomial function related to the output current,
f
I
out
.
(12)
η =
η
max
1 − exp
f
I
out
.

The authors in [32,33] propose another model, here called “Logarithmic”, in which the efficiency is only a function of the output current. The Logarithmic model is described with three parameters:
(13)
η =
η
max
+
1
4
η
max
−
η
0
log
I
out
I
η
max
2
,
where
η
max
, is the maximum efficiency,
I
max
the output current that maximizes the efficiency and
η
0
is the unknown parameter to be optimized. Both, the Experimental and the Logarithmic model reproduce quite well the behavior of the converters under low output currents, but show important errors for medium and high output currents (see Fig. 9). In [34] a review of efficiency models for power converters is presented. Among all models, the most promising ones are the bidimensional. These models are useful for applications in which the input voltage of the converters is variable, so the efficiency depends not only on the output power but on the operation point of the energy source. The first model, here called “2-parameter”, is developed in [35] and is derived from an equivalent electrical circuit. This model has two parameters to be estimated by curve fitting: a series and a parallel resistance,
R
s
and
R
p
respectively:
(14)
η =
2

2
R
p
,
where
P
out
is the output power. The results obtained with this model are not satisfactory (see Fig. 9). For medium and high output currents, the model reproduces correctly the efficiency of the converter. However, the errors are significant at low currents. The second model is proposed in [36] and is defined as:
(15)
η =
P

to be linear functions as proposed in [34]. Nevertheless, with this simplification, an amount of 6 parameters have to be estimated (two per function). As models of Eqs. (12)–(14) are not accurate throughout the range of the output current and the parameters of the model of Eq. (15) are complex to estimate, the authors of the present paper have developed the following simple and accurate model described by four parameters:
(16)

In this model, α reproduces the exponential and quasi-linear dependence of the efficiency with the output current, β and γ model the linear relation between the input voltage and the efficiency, and
I

represents the output current for maximum efficiency. Indeed, the first and second derivatives of Eq. (16) with respect to the output current provide the following expressions:
(17)

.

All of these parameters can be extracted by applying the Least Square Method (LSM) to the four test data. To initialize the optimization of the parameters, the initial guess of
I
η
max
, β and γ can be extracted directly from the experimental data. For example, the initial guess of
I
η
max
is 0.7 for the 5 V DC–DC converter as mentioned above. The parameter β is estimated considering the linear regression slope of the efficiency as a function of the input voltage for a particular output current (see Fig. 10). And finally, γ takes a minimum value for the minimum efficiency of the tests.

The parameters of the proposed model for both DC–DC converters are presented in Table 4. In general, the proposed model is very accurate, except for small deviations (see Fig. 8). The RMSE is very low, as shown in Table 5. Also, a quick comparison of each parameter between the converters shows that they are of the same order of magnitude. This fact leads to the idea that the proposed model is compatible with both converters and could be used in similar converters.

Once the main electrical components of the EPS have been modeled independently, it is time to integrate them into a single EPS model. Therefore, simulations can be performed to predict the performance of the EPS before advancing to its integration, allowing design changes if necessary.

The formulation of the EPS mathematical model mainly aims to predict, under any power demand, the voltage level of the battery and the power losses at any time. As the battery model presented relates the discharged energy with its voltage, it is necessary to know how the discharged energy evolves. All energy is provided by the battery, so the following initial value problem can be formulated:
(21)
d ϕ
d t
= P
t , ϕ
ϕ
t
0
=
ϕ
0
,
where the energy conservation equation is represented by the differential equation and states that the rate of change of the discharged energy, ϕ , is equal to the supplied power, P . Regarding the initial condition, it sets the discharging energy level at the beginning of the simulation and, thus, the initial battery voltage level. In order to solve the previous initial value problem, the supplied power has to be modeled. The main contributions of this power are the bus power,
P
bus
, and the battery losses,
P
b, losses
:
(22)
P =
P
bus
+
P
b, losses
.
The battery losses can be calculated with Joule’s law:
(23)
P
b, losses
=
I
2
R
,
and the bus power corresponds to the consumption power of the DC–DC converters. Calling
P
12 V
and
P
5 V
to the power output of the 12 V and 5 V converters and
η
12 V
and
η
5 V
to their efficiency, the power bus can be calculated as follows:
(24)
P
bus
=
P
12 V
η
12 V
+
P
5 V
η
5 V
,
where
P
12 V
is a function of mission time and
P
5 V
is constant (see Fig. 3). Regarding the efficiency of DC–DC converters,
η
12 V
and
η
5 V
are functions of the input voltage and the current consumption of the ＋12 V and ＋5 V lines (see Eq. (16) and Table 4). The input voltage of both converters is:
(25)
V
in
= V − I R
,
and the output currents of the 12 V and 5 V converters (
I
out
12V
and
I
out
5V
, respectively) are obtained as follows:
(26)
I
out
12V
=
P
12 V
12
,
(27)
I
out
5V
=
P
5 V
5
.
Then, the efficiencies of the DC–DC converters can be expressed as:
(28)
η
12 V
=
η
12 V
V − I R ,
I
out
12V
,
(29)
η
5 V
=
η
5 V
V − I R ,
I
out
5V
.

Now, it is evident that it is necessary to determine the current of the battery, I , at every integration step to calculate the power supplied and then solve (21). The power supplied can be expressed as:
(30)
P = V I
,
so Eq. (24) can be rewritten as the following non-linear equation:
(31)
V I −
P
12 V
η
12 V
V − I R ,
I
out
12V
−
P
5 V
η
5 V
V − I R ,
I
out
5V
−
I
2
R = 0
.

The initial value problem of (21) has been solved considering the power demand in Fig. 3 and setting the maximum battery voltage at the beginning of the simulation (
ϕ
0
= 0
). The evolution of the simulated battery voltage is plotted together with the experimental data in Fig. 12.

Before concluding the integration campaign of TASEC-Lab, its EPS was subjected to functional tests at the electronic laboratory of the IDR/UPM Institute. The last and most critical test consisted of connecting representative power loads, in intensity and duration, of the real mission to the EPS. These power loads, previously shown in Fig. 3, were emulated with two electronic programmable loads, one connected to the ＋5 V line and the other to the ＋12 V line. On the one hand, the power demand on the ＋5 V line, with an approximate constant power of 3.5 W was reproduced with the IT8512 A Programmable DC Electronic Load [37]. On the other hand, the power demand of the + 12 V line, composed of four intervals of approximately constant power (2.7 W, 2.5 W, 2 W and 1.5 W), was emulated with BK Precision 8500 Programmable Electronic Load [38]. Finally, the battery voltage was measured with the aforementioned Mayuno M9812, recording data every second. A simplified schematic of this functional test is shown in Fig. 11.

The functional test was successful, as the battery voltage level remained at a safe level after 8 h of test, as shown in Fig. 12. According to this figure, the numerical results agree with the test measurements. The accuracy of the simulation seems to be quite accurate during the first third of the mission time, the maximum deviation being less than 0.3 V. Furthermore, the most important fact is that at the end of the simulation, the battery voltage differs very little from the measured one. In addition, the losses of the power subsystem have been obtained from this simulation. In Fig. 13 the simulated power dissipation of the TASEC-Lab power subsystem is shown. According to the plotted results, it can be concluded that power losses are compressed between 600 and 750 mW. In addition, battery losses are negligible compared to the losses of the 12 V and 5 V converters. This is due to the fact that the battery discharging current is very small (
I ≈
0.3 A). However, battery losses have to be included in the thermal model of TASEC-Lab, as small heat sources could change its thermal behavior. The amount of battery energy discharged, ϕ , is around 47.71 W h, so there is still around 20% of the remaining battery energy. This value is large enough to feed an excess of unplanned consumption and small enough to avoid an oversizing of the battery. Finally, the power losses of the entire power subsystem are around 5.24 W h, making a global efficiency of 90%.

The purpose of this paper is to describe the modeling of the power subsystem of TASEC-Lab, a fully autonomous payload within a stratospheric balloon operated by B2Space. This balloon was launched from León (Spain) in July 2021. Several laboratory tests were performed to successfully model the behavior of the battery, the DC–DC converters, and the complete power system. The major conclusions resulting from this work are as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: A new model for Li-ion batteries discharging process has been proposed, being an original contribution. It showed good accuracy in relation to the experimental data, and it fitted the three parts of a Li-ion discharging curve (voltage as a function of the discharged energy): the initial one, exponential; the second one, linear; and the third one, exponential decay.
Additionally, the DC/DC converter model proposed in this paper is also an original contribution. It accurately fitted the behavior of the TASEC-Lab DC/DC converters.

Finally, the TASEC-Lab power subsystem was tested and simulated considering the power consumption profile of the mission. The results of the battery discharging process during the simulation matched well with the results measured during the test.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support of the IDR/UPM Institute staff and the students from UPM involved in the TASEC-Lab project. The authors thank B2Space for the opportunity to participate in the balloon flight. The authors are also grateful to the project 478 Y2020/NMT-6427 OAPES from the program “Sinérgicos 2020” from Comunidad de Madrid (Spain).
